points.text: COVID-19 spreads mainly through inhaling air contaminated by droplets and aerosols from infected people.
Infected individuals exhale virus particles when they breathe, talk, cough, sneeze, or sing.
Transmission is most likely at close range but can occur over longer distances indoors.
COVID-19 can spread by touching eyes, nose, or mouth after contact with contaminated surfaces.
People can spread the virus even if they do not develop symptoms and remain contagious for up to 20 days.
COVID-19 spreads mainly from person to person through the air in tiny droplets when people cough, sneeze, breathe, sing, or talk.
The virus can land directly on the face of a nearby person after a sneeze or cough, or be breathed in by others in close proximity or areas with low airflow.
Close contact, such as sitting next to someone or sharing a car, increases the risk of catching COVID-19.
Risk factors include living with someone who has COVID-19 and spending time in crowded spaces with poor airflow.
COVID-19 can spread from someone who is infected but has no symptoms.
COVID-19 spreads through droplets from an infected person's mouth or nose when they cough, sneeze, speak, sing, or breathe.
The virus can spread via larger respiratory droplets and smaller aerosols, and people can be contagious without symptoms.
COVID-19 can spread by touching the mouth, nose, or eyes after contacting contaminated surfaces.
Preventive measures include mask-wearing, hand washing, and avoiding close contact with symptomatic individuals.
Experts continue to research how COVID-19 spreads and identify high-risk situations.
